summary: Spolsky describes a visit to 7 World Trade Center where he encountered a new elevator system that uses numeric keypads in the lobby instead of traditional up/down buttons. The system optimizes elevator scheduling by grouping people going to similar floors together. However, he identifies a usability bug: newcomers see an open elevator door, jump in, and get stuck going to a random floor because there are no buttons inside. He draws a parallel to the 1950s movie 'The Apartment,' which accomplished the same optimization using human operators.
key_points: The new elevator system replaces traditional buttons with lobby keypads that assign you to a specific elevator, optimizing routing by grouping passengers going to similar floors
The critical usability flaw is that the system breaks a deeply ingrained mental model — people see an open elevator and step in, only to find they can't control where they're going
Efficiency gains from new technology can be undermined when the interface contradicts universal user expectations
The same optimization problem was solved decades ago with human operators, suggesting that some 'new' tech solutions are really old ideas with new interfaces
money_quotes: Instead of having up and down buttons outside the elevators, there's a numeric keypad, where you key in the floor number you're going to.
This is more efficient than the old system, in which two people who were going to the same floor might have taken separate elevators, adding an unnecessary trip.

People who aren't used to the new system come into the lobby and see an elevator with an open door. They jump into it, and then get stuck going to some random floor because they can't key in their destination once they're inside.

Presumably, during the early morning rush, it is able to clump people going to nearby floors into the same elevator, thus getting more people to their destinations faster by intelligently optimizing elevator schedules on-the-fly.
Instead of letting any arbitrary person force any arbitrary elevator to take them to any arbitrary floor.
key_insight: When a new system is more efficient but violates deeply ingrained user expectations, the usability failure can completely negate the engineering improvement.
